# EDA findings — dengue × climate × elevation

*Generated by `analysis/eda.py` from `analysis_panel.csv`
(2772 district-months, 77 districts,
2022–2024).*

## 1. Where cases concentrate

| band | total_cases | share_pct |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Terai | 40848 | 29.1 |
| Hill | 93000 | 66.2 |
| Mountain | 6564 | 4.7 |

Cases are not spread evenly across the elevation gradient — the
**Hill** belt carries the largest share
(66.2%). Heaviest districts:

Kathmandu (22,371), Sunsari (16,891), Lalitpur (11,599), Kaski (11,006), Tanahun (10,103), Bhaktapur (8,038), Makwanpur (6,474), Dhading (5,222), Chitwan (4,877), Jhapa (4,368).

## 2. Seasonality

Peak month of mean cases — overall: **Sep**;
Terai: Sep; Hill: Sep; Mountain: Oct.
The outbreak is a sharp Jul–Oct surge (see
`cases_climate_overlay.png` and `seasonality_by_band.png`).

## 3. Cases vs climate (Pearson r)

Overall correlation of monthly cases with each climate variable:

| variable | r_overall |
| --- | --- |
| precip | 0.09 |
| temp_mean | 0.07 |
| temp_max | 0.04 |
| temp_min | 0.1 |
| humidity | 0.14 |

By band (does the climate–cases link change with elevation?):

| variable | Terai | Hill | Mountain |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| precip | 0.18 | 0.05 | 0.1 |
| temp_mean | 0.05 | 0.08 | 0.18 |
| temp_max | -0.01 | 0.04 | 0.15 |
| temp_min | 0.09 | 0.11 | 0.2 |
| humidity | 0.16 | 0.16 | 0.22 |

The strongest single contemporaneous association is **humidity**
(r = 0.14). Same-month correlations are modest, which is
expected: climate is thought to *lead* cases by 1–2 months — quantified in the
lag analysis, not here.

## 4. Distribution & overdispersion

| group | mean | variance | var_mean_ratio | pct_zero |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| All | 50.7 | 125674 | 2481 | 36.7 |
| Terai | 54 | 134410 | 2487.6 | 27.4 |
| Hill | 64.6 | 169847 | 2629.9 | 35.7 |
| Mountain | 11.4 | 2047.9 | 179.7 | 51.4 |

Monthly counts are **zero-heavy** (36.7% of district-months are zero) and
**overdispersed**: the variance is 2481× the mean overall. This rules out
Poisson and motivates the **negative-binomial** model used downstream.

## 5. Implications for modeling

- Use a **negative-binomial** GLM (overdispersion above).
- Test **lagged** climate predictors (same-month r is weak; see the lag analysis).
- Keep an **elevation-band × climate** interaction: the per-band correlations
  above differ, hinting the climate response is not uniform across the gradient.
- No anomalies found that require new cleaning rules; the panel is complete
  (no nulls, expected row count) from the panel build.
